sections.heading: Architecture Overview
sections.content:
The PEV Agent Nexus is three Claude Code agent definitions orchestrated by a skill (`/pev`), each with structurally enforced tool permissions. Claude owns reasoning within each phase. Cortex MCP tools own the knowledge layer. The orchestrator skill owns phase transitions, human gates, tag management, and the cycle manifest.

```
User request
  → Orchestrator creates cycle manifest doc (pev-active tag)
  → Architect (doc-write to cycle manifest only, no code, no feature docs)
    → explore codebase → return NEEDS_INPUT with questions
    → Orchestrator relays via AskUserQuestion → resumes Architect with answers
    → (repeat if more questions)
    → scope decision → write Shape Up pitch to cycle manifest
    → Architect validation (lightweight reviewer, max 2 loops)
    → Human gate
  → Builder (code-write, doc-read, worktree + cortex checkout)
    → read pitch → decompose work → TDD → manifest written to cycle manifest
    → Worktree merge (surface conflicts, never auto-resolve)
    → cortex_build + cortex_check on main
    → Human gate
  → Auditor (doc-write to feature docs, code-read, mark_clean)
    → cortex_check for stale nodes → cross-ref Builder's change-set → review
    → update live feature docs → impact report
    → owns post-implementation protocol (PRD updates, design spec, links, checkpoint)
    → needs_fix? → Builder loopback (worktree, max 2x) → Auditor re-check
    → Human gate
  → Commit (one commit, everything together)
```

**Planning model: Shape Up over waterfall.** The Architect writes a pitch — problem, user stories (3-5 coarse outcomes), solution sketch (fat-marker approach at module level), and constraints (rabbit holes and no-gos). The Builder receives orientation and boundaries, not a prescriptive implementation spec. The Builder reads source code, decomposes the work, and makes implementation decisions. This follows the Shape Up (Basecamp) model: define appetite and boundaries, trust the builder.

**Key change from original design:** The Architect writes pitches into an isolated cycle manifest doc — not into live feature docs. Feature docs are ground truth for what EXISTS, and are only updated by the Auditor after code is implemented and merged. This eliminates doc contention between parallel PEV cycles and prevents feature docs from describing unbuilt functionality.

**Auditor scope is empirical.** The Auditor determines review scope from `cortex_check` (what's stale) and the Builder's change-set (what actually changed), not from the Architect's predictions. The Architect's coarse scope boundary serves only as a sanity check.

**User interaction via proxy:** Subagents cannot call `AskUserQuestion` directly (platform limitation — deferred tools not resolved for subagents). The Architect uses a proxy-question protocol: it returns `NEEDS_INPUT` JSON, the orchestrator relays to the user, and resumes the agent with answers. See ADR-012 for the full protocol specification.

**Why Claude Code native over LangGraph:** Eliminates LangGraph, Anthropic API, and checkpoint DB dependencies. Agent definitions with `tools:` allowlists provide runtime-enforced tool restriction. Per-agent `hooks:` frontmatter provides defense-in-depth. Trade-off: no programmatic checkpoint/resume across process restarts — the cycle manifest doc provides resume capability instead.

sections.heading: Tool Permissions Matrix
sections.content:
Structural enforcement: each agent definition's `tools:` frontmatter strips unauthorized tools at runtime. The agent literally cannot see or call tools not in its allowlist. Verified empirically — see ADR-012 Phase 0 spike results.

| Capability | Architect | Builder | Auditor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Read code (`cortex_source`, `cortex_render`) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Read docs (`cortex_read_doc`) | Yes | Yes (read-only) | Yes |
| Search/graph (`cortex_search`, `cortex_graph`) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Write code (`Edit`, `Write`, `Bash`) | **No** | Yes | **No** |
| Write docs (`cortex_update_section`, `cortex_write_doc`, `cortex_add_section`) | Yes¹ | **No**² | Yes |
| Add links (`cortex_add_link`) | **No** | **No** | **Yes** |
| Mark clean (`cortex_mark_clean`) | **No** | **No** | **Yes** |
| Manage tags | **No** | **No** | **Yes** (remove only) |
| Build index (`cortex_build`) | Yes | **No** | Yes |
| User interaction | Proxy³ | **No** | **No** |

**Invariant:** No single agent can both write code AND update documentation.

**¹ Architect doc-write scope:** The Architect has doc-write tools but is structurally restricted to the cycle manifest doc. A Layer 2 PreToolUse hook checks the `doc_id` argument of `cortex_update_section`, `cortex_write_doc`, and `cortex_add_section` against `cycle_doc_id` from `.claude/pev-state.json` (written by the orchestrator before dispatch). Calls targeting any other doc are rejected (exit 2). This makes the "cycle manifest only" constraint structural, not prompt-enforced.

**² Builder manifest writing:** The Builder has no doc-write tools. It returns its implementation manifest as structured data in the subagent completion message. The Orchestrator receives the result and writes the `builder` section to the cycle manifest via `cortex_update_section`. This preserves the "no doc-write" invariant while ensuring the manifest is recorded.

**³ Proxy-question protocol:** `AskUserQuestion` is not available in subagents (platform limitation — deferred tools are not resolved for subagents, regression since Claude Code v2.0.56). The Architect returns `NEEDS_INPUT` JSON payloads; the orchestrator relays questions to the user via `AskUserQuestion` and resumes the Architect with answers via `SendMessage`. See ADR-012 decision section for full protocol spec.

**Tag management:** The orchestrator sets `planned` and `in_progress` tags. Only the Auditor removes tags (after `mark_clean`). The orchestrator sets status to `incomplete` on failure.

sections.heading: Tag State Machine
sections.content:
Tags live on the **cycle manifest doc** (not on shared feature docs). They are queryable via `cortex_list(tag=...)` and visible in the viz.

```
                  Architect
  (no tag) ──────────────────→ planned
                                  │
                           Orchestrator
                           (Builder dispatch)
                                  │
                                  ▼
                             in_progress
                              │       │
                    Auditor   │       │  Orchestrator
                  (success)   │       │  (failure)
                              ▼       ▼
                          (removed)  (stays in_progress,
                                      status → incomplete)
```

**Tags are per-cycle:** Because each cycle has its own manifest doc, parallel PEV cycles have independent tag sets. No contention.

**Two-action completion:** The Auditor performs `mark_clean` (confirms content accuracy) and then removes the tag (confirms PEV cycle completion) as separate operations. This prevents rubber-stamping — each action is a distinct judgment.

**Failure semantics:** When the Builder fails or times out, the orchestrator updates the cycle manifest `status` to `incomplete`. The `pev-active` tag stays — it means "this cycle hasn't been closed out." The resume flow finds incomplete cycles via `cortex_list(tag="pev-active")` and reads the `status` section to distinguish active from incomplete. Only completion and explicit release remove `pev-active`.

**`pev-active` tag:** Set on the cycle manifest doc by the orchestrator at cycle start. Removed only on successful completion or explicit user release. Used by the resume flow to find both active and interrupted cycles.

sections.heading: Cycle Manifest Doc
sections.content:
The cycle manifest is the single artifact that tracks a PEV cycle's full lifecycle. It holds the plan, records phase completion, and enables resume after interruption.

### Location & Naming

```
docs/pev-cycles/{cycle-id}.json     (DocJSON format)
```

Cycle ID format: `pev-YYYY-MM-DD-{slugified-purpose}` (e.g., `pev-2026-03-21-add-history-filtering`). The slug is derived from the user's request: lowercase, hyphens, truncated to 40 characters. Generated by the orchestrator at `/pev` start. The user confirms the cycle identity before the Architect starts.

Uniqueness: the orchestrator checks `docs/pev-cycles/` for existing files with the same slug. If a collision exists, appends `-2`, `-3`, etc.

### Sections

| Section ID | Filled by | When | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `status` | Orchestrator | Created at start, updated at each phase transition | Current phase, `baseline_sha` (main HEAD at cycle start), timestamps, and overall cycle state |
| `request` | Orchestrator | Created at start | Original user prompt (verbatim) |
| `scope` | Architect | During planning phase | Affected node IDs and what will change |
| `architect` | Architect | Planning complete | Scoped PRD + design spec for this cycle (see Architect Output Structure below) |
| `change-set` | Orchestrator | After Builder merge | Planned nodes (from Architect scope) + actual files changed (from `git diff`) + Builder deviations. Auditor uses this to categorize stale nodes as expected vs collateral. |
| `builder` | Orchestrator (from Builder return) | Implementation complete | Implementation manifest (deviations, files changed, tests). The Builder returns this as structured data; the orchestrator writes it. |
| `auditor` | Auditor | Validation complete | Impact report (reviewed, marked clean, flagged items) |

### Architect Output Structure

The `architect` section is a scoped PRD and design spec for this PEV cycle. It contains the same structure as the project's feature docs, but narrowed to only what this cycle will build. Sub-sections:

| Sub-section | Content |
|---|---|
| `architect.problem` | Problem statement — what is being solved and why |
| `architect.user-stories` | User stories scoped to this cycle (As a..., I want..., so that...) |
| `architect.capabilities` | Capabilities table with target status (Not started → planned). This is the Builder's checklist — each capability becomes a deliverable |
| `architect.design` | Technical design: architecture decisions, data model changes, component interactions, edge cases |
| `architect.constraints` | Non-negotiable constraints and trade-offs for this cycle |
| `architect.affected-nodes` | Cortex node IDs that will be modified, with what changes are expected per node |
| `architect.changelog-draft` | Draft changelog entry with `status: "planned"` linking affected nodes |

The Architect writes this as a self-contained spec that the Builder can read without needing to consult the live feature docs. The Builder reads from the cycle manifest, not from feature docs.

After implementation, the Auditor uses the capabilities table to update the live feature docs — marking capabilities as Done in the actual PRD.

### Who Fills Out What and When

**Orchestrator** — creates the doc and owns lifecycle transitions:

| When | Action |
|---|---|
| `/pev` starts | Create cycle manifest doc with `status` (including `baseline_sha` = main HEAD, started timestamp) and `request` sections. User confirms cycle identity. |
| `/pev` starts (if stale cycles exist) | Query for docs tagged `pev-active`; present to user: resume, release, or start new |
| Before Architect dispatch | Tag cycle manifest `pev-active`. Pass the cycle manifest doc ID in the Architect dispatch prompt. Set `PEV_CYCLE_DOC_ID` env var for the Architect's doc-scope hook. |
| On human approval of any phase | Update `status` section to next phase **immediately** (first action after approval, before any other work). This is the resume recovery mechanism. |
| Before Builder dispatch | Update `status` section to `phase: builder`. Create worktree via `git worktree add`. Run `cortex_checkout`. Dispatch Builder to worktree path. |
| After Builder completes | Write Builder's returned manifest to the `builder` section. Merge worktree. Remove worktree. |
| After Builder merge | Run `cortex_build` + `cortex_check` on main. Construct `change-set` section from Architect scope + `git diff --name-only {baseline_sha}..HEAD` + Builder deviations. |
| Before Auditor dispatch | Check `cortex_list(tag="pev-active")` for other cycles in phase `auditor`. If found, inform user and wait. Update `status` to `phase: auditor`. |
| On Builder failure | Update `status` section to `phase: incomplete`; remove `pev-active` tag |
| After Auditor completes | Update `status` section to `phase: commit-ready` |
| After final commit | Update `status` to `phase: completed` with commit SHA; remove `pev-active` tag |

**Architect** — fills out `scope` and `architect` sections:

| When | Action |
|---|---|
| Phase start | Read existing code + docs via cortex MCP tools. Orchestrator passes the cycle manifest doc ID in the dispatch prompt. |
| Scope decision | Write `scope` section: list affected node IDs, what each needs |
| Planning complete | Write `architect` section: scoped PRD + design spec (problem, user stories, capabilities table, technical design, constraints, affected nodes, changelog draft) |
| **Does NOT touch** | Live feature docs (PRD, design spec, capabilities table). Layer 2 hook enforces: doc-write calls targeting any doc other than the cycle manifest are rejected. |

**Builder** — returns implementation manifest (orchestrator writes `builder` section):

| When | Action |
|---|---|
| Phase start | Read `architect` section from cycle manifest (orchestrator passes doc ID in dispatch prompt) |
| During implementation | Uses cortex read tools from the worktree DB copy for context. Works through the capabilities table as a checklist. |
| Implementation complete | Return implementation manifest as structured data in subagent completion message (deviations, files changed, tests added, results). The orchestrator writes this to the `builder` section. |
| **Does NOT touch** | The cortex DB (read-only), any doc files, any feature docs. Has no doc-write tools. |

**Auditor** — fills out `auditor` section and updates live feature docs:

| When | Action |
|---|---|
| Phase start | Run `cortex_build` on merged main, then `cortex_check`. Read `change-set` section to understand expected vs collateral scope. |
| Staleness review | Review every stale node; mark clean or flag for fix. Categorize each as `expected` (in change-set) or `collateral` (from external merges or indirect effects). |
| Feature doc updates | Update live feature docs: capabilities table (status to Done per cycle manifest), design spec, interface specs |
| Doc-to-code links | `cortex_add_link` for new public entry points |
| Audit checks | Test, logging, workflow marker audits per Auditor Reference Protocol |
| Checkpoint | `cortex history checkpoint` with cycle ID in message |
| Impact report | Write `auditor` section in cycle manifest |
| **Owns post-implementation** | The Auditor IS the post-implementation protocol — no separate step |

### Resume Flow

On `/pev` start, the orchestrator checks for existing active cycles:

```
cortex_list(tag="pev-active")
  → if found:
    "Active PEV cycles:
      1. pev-2026-03-21-add-history-filtering: 'Add history filtering' — phase: builder
      2. pev-2026-03-20-fix-staleness-engine: 'Fix staleness engine' — phase: auditor
     Resume (1/2), release, or start new?"
  → Resume: read cycle manifest, skip completed phases, pick up at current phase
  → Release: remove pev-active tag, update status to incomplete
  → New: create fresh cycle manifest
```

The orchestrator determines what to skip by reading the `status` section and checking which sections have content:

- `status: builder` + empty `builder` section → Builder hasn't started, dispatch Builder
- `status: builder` + filled `builder` section → Builder finished but merge didn't happen, proceed to merge
- `status: auditor` + empty `auditor` section → Auditor hasn't started, dispatch Auditor (Auditor's opening `cortex_build` handles any missed post-merge indexing)
- `status: planning` + filled `architect` section → Plan wasn't approved yet, re-present for human approval
- `status: planning` + empty `architect` section → Architect hasn't started, dispatch Architect

### Parallel Cycle Isolation

Each cycle has its own manifest doc. No two cycles write to the same document. The Architect does not touch shared feature docs — plans stay in the cycle manifest (enforced by Layer 2 hook). Code conflicts between parallel Builders are handled by git merge. The Auditor runs on main after merge, so feature doc updates are sequential.

**Auditor sequencing:** Before dispatching an Auditor, the orchestrator checks `cortex_list(tag="pev-active")` for other cycles in phase `auditor`. If another Auditor is active on main, the orchestrator informs the user and waits for it to complete. This is enforced in the orchestrator skill — the orchestrator is the only entity that dispatches Auditors, so it's a single point of enforcement.

No locking, reservation, or merge-check needed. Existing tools handle conflicts:
- **Code files** — git merge (human resolves conflicts)
- **Feature docs** — only touched by the Auditor on main, sequentially (orchestrator enforces)
- **Cortex DB** — `cortex build` + `cortex check` detect all staleness after merge

sections.heading: cortex_checkout Tool
sections.content:
Provides the Builder with a consistent, read-only snapshot of the cortex index in an isolated worktree. Uses SQLite's `VACUUM INTO` for atomic copying — safe regardless of WAL state or concurrent writes.

### Interface

**MCP tool:**
```
cortex_checkout(project_root: str, worktree_path: str) -> str
```

**CLI:**
```
cortex checkout <worktree-path>
```

### Behavior

1. Validate `project_root/.cortex/cortex.db` exists
2. Validate `worktree_path` exists and is a directory (intentionally does not verify git worktree status — any directory is a valid target)
3. Create `worktree_path/.cortex/` if needed
4. Execute `VACUUM INTO 'worktree_path/.cortex/cortex.db'` on the source DB connection — this produces an atomic, consistent snapshot including any uncommitted WAL contents
5. If target DB already exists: skip with warning ("DB already exists, skipping — delete manually to force refresh")
6. Return success message with path

### Why VACUUM INTO

A raw file copy of an SQLite database in WAL mode can produce a corrupt or stale snapshot — the copy might miss uncommitted WAL contents. `VACUUM INTO` reads through the WAL, applies pending changes, and writes a clean, self-contained `.db` file. One SQL statement, atomic, no locking issues.

### What It Does NOT Do

- Does not modify the source DB
- Does not run `cortex build` in the worktree
- Does not import anything back (the worktree DB is read-only during Builder work)
- Does not verify that `worktree_path` is a git worktree — any directory is valid

### Worktree Lifecycle (Orchestrator-Owned)

The orchestrator owns the full worktree lifecycle. The Builder is NOT dispatched with `isolation: "worktree"` — the orchestrator creates and manages the worktree directly:

1. `git worktree add -b pev/{cycle-id} pev-worktrees/{cycle-id} HEAD` — orchestrator creates the worktree on a dedicated branch
2. `cortex_checkout(project_root, worktree_path)` — orchestrator copies the DB via VACUUM INTO
3. Dispatch Builder subagent pointed at the worktree path (no `isolation: "worktree"`)
4. Builder implements, tests, and commits on the worktree branch (`git add -A && git commit`)
5. Builder completes → orchestrator merges via `git merge --no-commit --no-ff pev/{cycle-id}`
6. Orchestrator removes worktree via `git worktree remove` and branch via `git branch -d`

This sequence ensures `cortex_checkout` runs after the worktree exists but before the Builder starts — no timing paradox. The Builder commits on the worktree branch so that `git merge` can reference the changes (without a commit, the worktree would have only uncommitted modifications and merge would fail).

sections.heading: Context Leash
sections.content:
Subagents degrade as their context fills — attention drifts, instructions get lost, output quality drops. The context leash is a deterministic, hook-driven mechanism that checkpoints a subagent before this happens and redeploys a fresh incarnation to continue.

### Mechanism

A PostToolUse hook on every PEV subagent tracks cumulative tool I/O bytes in a temporary state file. When the running total crosses a threshold, the hook injects a wrap-up message. The agent returns its progress summary to the orchestrator with status `CONTINUING`. The orchestrator writes the checkpoint to the cycle manifest and dispatches a fresh agent.

```
Agent works normally
  → PostToolUse hook fires after each tool call
  → Hook reads payload from stdin (JSON with tool_input + tool_response)
  → Hook extracts agent_id, measures payload bytes, updates /tmp state file
  → Total crosses context limit?
    → Hook outputs wrap-up message to agent
  → Agent summarizes progress and returns CONTINUING
  → Orchestrator writes checkpoint to cycle manifest
  → Orchestrator dispatches fresh agent: "Continue from checkpoint in {doc_id}"
  → Fresh agent reads checkpoint, picks up where previous left off
```

### Configuration

The orchestrator writes `.claude/pev-state.json` before each subagent dispatch:

```json
{
  "cycle_id": "pev-2026-03-21-add-history-filtering",
  "cycle_doc_id": "cortex::docs.pev-cycles.pev-2026-03-21-add-history-filtering",
  "context_limit": 100000
}
```

The cycle doc ID is also passed in the dispatch prompt (so the agent knows which doc to read). The state file is for hooks that can't receive prompt context.

### The Hook

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# context-leash.sh — PostToolUse hook for all PEV subagents
# Reads config from .claude/pev-state.json (written by orchestrator before dispatch)

# Capture full stdin payload (JSON with tool_input + tool_response + metadata)
INPUT=$(cat)

# Extract agent_id from hook payload (unique per dispatch, no collision)
AGENT_ID=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.agent_id')

# Read context limit from orchestrator state file
LIMIT=$(jq -r '.context_limit // 100000' .claude/pev-state.json 2>/dev/null)

# State file scoped by agent_id — unique per dispatch, resets per incarnation
STATE_FILE="/tmp/pev-leash-${AGENT_ID}"

# Measure payload size (tool input + output + metadata)
PAYLOAD_SIZE=${#INPUT}

CURRENT=0
[ -f "$STATE_FILE" ] && CURRENT=$(cat "$STATE_FILE")
TOTAL=$((CURRENT + PAYLOAD_SIZE))
echo "$TOTAL" > "$STATE_FILE"

if [ "$TOTAL" -gt "$LIMIT" ]; then
  cat <<'MSG'
CONTEXT LEASH REACHED. You must immediately:
1. Summarize your progress (what's done, in progress, remaining, decisions made)
2. Return to the orchestrator with status CONTINUING
Do not start new work.
MSG
fi
```

**Why `agent_id` instead of env vars:** Claude Code's PostToolUse hook payload includes `agent_id` — a unique identifier per subagent dispatch. Each dispatch gets a different `agent_id`, so the state file is automatically scoped per-agent-instance with no collision. No env var plumbing is needed. Verified empirically via Phase 0 spike (see ADR-012 implementation approach).

**Why no env vars:** `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` is not available in SubagentStart hook context (verified empirically). Env vars cannot be injected into subagents at dispatch time. The state file approach (`.claude/pev-state.json`) is the working alternative.

The hook is identical for all three agents. It fires deterministically — no prompt-based enforcement.

### Thresholds

The orchestrator writes `context_limit` to `.claude/pev-state.json` before each subagent dispatch. Starting values are conservative — start low, raise only if you see too many unnecessary incarnations:

| Model | context_limit | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Opus | 100000 | Start conservative, tune up if quality is still sharp at checkpoint |
| Sonnet | 60000 | Smaller effective window |
| Haiku | 40000 | Smallest effective window |

These are byte counts of cumulative tool I/O, not total context tokens. Tool I/O is the dominant but not sole source of context growth (agent reasoning is unmeasured). The conservative thresholds account for this gap.

**Tuning:** Start conservative. If agents checkpoint too frequently (many incarnations per phase), raise the limit. If output quality degrades near checkpoints, lower it. The cycle manifest records incarnation counts for visibility.

### Incarnation Contract

All subagents follow the same contract when the leash fires:

1. Stop current work immediately
2. Return a structured progress summary to the orchestrator with status `CONTINUING`
3. The orchestrator writes the checkpoint to the cycle manifest
4. The orchestrator dispatches a fresh incarnation with the cycle manifest doc ID

The agent does NOT write the checkpoint itself (consistent with the Builder contract — agents return data, orchestrator writes it). The fresh incarnation reads the checkpoint from the cycle manifest before starting any work.

**Builder incarnations** reuse the same worktree — code on disk is the state, nothing is lost. The checkpoint tells the fresh Builder what capabilities are done, in progress, and remaining.

**Auditor incarnations** benefit most from this — reviewing many stale nodes is the biggest context consumer. The checkpoint records which nodes were already reviewed and their dispositions. Already-marked-clean nodes won't appear stale, so the fresh Auditor naturally skips them.

**Architect incarnations** are least likely to fire but supported. The checkpoint records scope decisions and which sub-sections of the architect plan are written.

### Edge Cases

**Agent ignores the message:** The hook fires again on every subsequent tool call (total stays above threshold). If the agent still doesn't comply, the orchestrator can set `maxTurns` as a secondary leash.

**Multiple incarnations:** Each incarnation gets a new `agent_id` (assigned by Claude Code at dispatch), so each has its own state file starting at 0. The orchestrator tracks incarnation count in the cycle manifest `status` section. If an agent hits 5+ incarnations, surface to user — something may be wrong with scope.

**Checkpoint quality:** The orchestrator receives the agent's return and writes it. If the summary is too sparse, the orchestrator can ask the user before redeploying. The cycle manifest is always the source of truth — the fresh incarnation reads it, not a prompt blob.

sections.heading: Architect Output
sections.content:
The Architect produces a **scoped PRD + design spec** in the cycle manifest's `architect` section. This is a self-contained spec with the same structure as the project's feature docs, narrowed to this cycle:

- **Problem statement** — what is being solved and why
- **User stories** — scoped to this cycle (As a..., I want..., so that...)
- **Capabilities table** — each capability the Builder must deliver, with target status. This is the Builder's checklist.
- **Technical design** — architecture decisions, data model changes, component interactions, edge cases
- **Constraints** — non-negotiable constraints and trade-offs for this cycle
- **Affected nodes** — cortex node IDs that will be modified, with expected changes per node
- **Changelog draft** — draft changelog entry with `status: "planned"` linking affected nodes

The Architect also writes the `scope` section (affected node IDs and what each needs).

The Architect makes a scope decision first: single-feature change → plan targets that feature tree. Cross-cutting change (3+ features) → plan references or proposes an ADR.

**The Architect does NOT touch live feature docs** (PRD, design spec, capabilities table, interface specs). The cycle manifest is a self-contained spec that the Builder reads directly. The Auditor reconciles the cycle manifest's capabilities table with the live feature docs after implementation.

sections.heading: Builder Output (Implementation Manifest)
sections.content:
The Builder produces code changes, commits them in the worktree branch, and returns its implementation manifest as structured data in the subagent completion message. The Orchestrator receives the result and writes it to the `builder` section in the cycle manifest via `cortex_update_section`.

**Builder return format:**

```json
{
  "changelog_entry": "cortex::docs.features.{name}.changelog::YYYY-MM-DD-slug",
  "deviations": [
    {
      "spec_requirement": "what was planned",
      "actual": "what was built",
      "reason": "why it changed",
      "affected_nodes": ["cortex::node_ids"]
    }
  ],
  "files_changed": ["path/to/file.py"],
  "tests_added": ["tests/path/to/test.py"],
  "tests_passed": true
}
```

The Builder reads the `architect` section from the cycle manifest (orchestrator passes the doc ID in the dispatch prompt). It uses cortex read tools (`cortex_source`, `cortex_search`, `cortex_graph`, `cortex_read_doc`) from a worktree DB copy (provided by `cortex_checkout`) for codebase context.

The Builder runs in an isolated git worktree created by the orchestrator on a dedicated branch (`pev/{cycle-id}`). Before returning, the Builder commits all changes (`git add -A && git commit`) on this branch — this commit is a transport mechanism that allows the orchestrator to merge via `git merge --no-commit --no-ff pev/{cycle-id}`. The Builder does NOT push, create the final commit, modify the cortex DB (read-only copy), write to any documentation files, or call any doc-write MCP tools.

sections.heading: Auditor Output (Impact Report)
sections.content:
The Auditor writes the `auditor` section in the cycle manifest and updates live feature docs:

**Cycle manifest `auditor` section (Impact Report):**

```json
{
  "iterations": 1,
  "stale_reviewed": 8,
  "docs_updated": [{"section_id": "...", "reason": "..."}],
  "marked_clean": [
    {
      "node_id": "...",
      "type": "code|doc|test",
      "scope": "expected|collateral",
      "reason": "..."
    }
  ],
  "needs_fix": [
    {
      "node_id": "...",
      "category": "code_bug|needs_new_tests|test_budget|logging|workflow_markers",
      "scope": "expected|collateral",
      "reason": "specific diagnosis",
      "suggested_fix": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "tags_removed": ["node_ids where tag was removed after mark_clean"],
  "errors": []
}
```

**Scope categorization:** Each `marked_clean` and `needs_fix` entry includes a `scope` field:
- **`expected`** — the node is in the `change-set` (planned by Architect, or in files the Builder actually changed). Direct result of this PEV cycle.
- **`collateral`** — the node is stale but not in the `change-set`. Caused by external merges between sessions, indirect effects, or unrelated changes. Flagged for awareness; the user decides whether to address or defer.

The Auditor reads the `change-set` section (constructed by the orchestrator after Builder merge) to make this categorization.

**Live feature doc updates (post-implementation protocol):**

The Auditor IS the post-implementation protocol — there is no separate step. After reviewing stale nodes, the Auditor:

1. Updates the sub-feature PRD capabilities table (status to Done for completed capabilities)
2. Updates interface specs if CLI/API shapes changed
3. Updates the design spec if architecture changed
4. Adds doc-to-code links via `cortex_add_link` for new public entry points
5. Runs `cortex_list_undocumented` to find and link undocumented nodes
6. Creates a checkpoint: `cortex history checkpoint` with cycle ID in message

`needs_fix` items with categories `test_budget`, `logging`, or `workflow_markers` come from the Auditor review checks. These trigger Builder loopback if actionable.

sections.heading: Auditor Review Responsibilities
sections.content:
The Auditor's review goes beyond basic staleness. It encompasses eight checks, detailed in the PRD. Three are new additions:

**Logging audit (ADR-014):** For each modified code node, the Auditor reads the source and judges whether it needs logging per ADR-014 patterns — tool entry/exit timing, phase milestones, exception handler visibility. This is a judgment call, not a mechanical grep. Findings go in the Impact Report under `needs_fix` with category `logging`.

**Test annotation audit:** The Auditor checks tests added by the Builder against the test annotation policy. It verifies tier assignment (Tier 1/2/3) is correct and flags excessive test counts relative to change scope. The test budget guideline is 5–10 focused tests per subsystem change; past 15 likely means testing implementation details. Findings use category `test_budget`.

**Workflow step markers:** Same pattern as before — check if key multi-step functions have accurate dFlow markers. Findings use category `workflow_markers`.

sections.heading: Implementation Constraints
sections.content:
These constraints are non-negotiable. Everything else is left to the implementer's judgment.

1. **Two-layer enforcement** — Layer 1: agent `tools:` allowlist (runtime strips — verified empirically). Layer 2: per-agent `hooks:` PreToolUse (exit 2 blocks — verified empirically). No tool restriction relies on prompts alone.
2. **Architect doc-write scoped to cycle manifest** — Layer 2 PreToolUse hook checks the `doc_id` argument against `cycle_doc_id` from `.claude/pev-state.json` (written by orchestrator before dispatch). Calls to `cortex_update_section`, `cortex_write_doc`, or `cortex_add_section` targeting any other doc are rejected (exit 2).
3. **No single agent writes both code and docs** — structural invariant.
4. **Builder returns manifest, orchestrator writes it** — The Builder has no doc-write tools. It returns its implementation manifest in the subagent result. The orchestrator writes it to the cycle manifest.
5. **Only the Auditor marks clean and removes tags** — two separate actions.
6. **One commit at the end** — code + docs + changelog together, only after human approves. The Builder commits in its worktree branch as a transport mechanism for `git merge`, but the orchestrator creates the final commit.
7. **Merge conflicts surfaced, never auto-resolved** — user decides.
8. **No `cortex init` during workflow** — staleness resolved through `mark_clean`, not reset.
9. **All docs are DocJSON** — written via Cortex MCP tools, never Edit/Write on JSON files in `docs/`.
10. **Feature docs are ground truth for what EXISTS** — only the Auditor updates them, and only after code is implemented and merged. The Architect writes plans to the cycle manifest, not to feature docs.
11. **Cycle manifest isolation** — each PEV cycle writes to its own manifest doc. No two cycles share a manifest. Parallel cycles do not contend on documentation.
12. **Orchestrator owns worktree lifecycle** — the orchestrator creates the worktree on a dedicated branch (`git worktree add -b pev/{cycle-id} pev-worktrees/{cycle-id} HEAD`), runs `cortex_checkout` (VACUUM INTO), dispatches the Builder to that path, merges the branch after approval (`git merge --no-commit --no-ff pev/{cycle-id}`), and removes the worktree and branch. The Builder is NOT dispatched with `isolation: "worktree"`. (Note: Claude Code's built-in `isolation: worktree` exists but doesn't copy the cortex DB — verified empirically. The orchestrator manages the worktree to ensure `cortex_checkout` runs between creation and dispatch.)
13. **Baseline SHA in manifest** — the orchestrator records main HEAD at cycle start in the `status` section. All subsequent diffs reference this SHA.
14. **Context leash on all subagents** — PostToolUse hook tracks cumulative tool I/O bytes in a `/tmp/pev-leash-{agent_id}` state file (agent_id from hook payload, unique per dispatch). Fires deterministically at a configurable threshold read from `.claude/pev-state.json`. Agent returns `CONTINUING`, orchestrator writes checkpoint to cycle manifest, dispatches fresh incarnation.
15. **No env vars for hook configuration** — `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` is not available in SubagentStart hook context (verified empirically). All hook configuration reads from `.claude/pev-state.json` (written by orchestrator before dispatch). Cycle doc ID is passed in the dispatch prompt.

**Linked nodes:**
- `cortex::docs.adr-012-pev-agent-system-claude-code-native-implementation::decision` — Decision
